executive_brief:
The threat landscape has fundamentally fractured. Artificial intelligence has collapsed the traditional vulnerability lifecycle, compressing the time-to-exploit (TTE) into negative territory where weaponization consistently precedes vendor patching. This reality forced India's CERT-In to mandate a 12-hour containment window for internet-facing flaws, acknowledging that legacy 30-to-45-day patch SLAs are functionally obsolete. Attackers are no longer waiting for human-led triage; AI-driven scanners and automated exploit generators are chaining misconfigurations and unpatched CVEs at machine speed, turning perimeter defense into a losing game of whack-a-mole.

Simultaneously, the software supply chain has become a primary battlefield. Threat actors have shifted from targeting end-user applications to poisoning the developer tooling, CI/CD pipelines, and package registries that build them. Git tag rewriting, compromised CI runners, and trojanized IDE extensions are delivering credential harvesters directly into enterprise build environments. Defenders who only scan production artifacts are blind to infections that occur at commit time.

The final paradigm shift is the systematic retirement of multifactor authentication as a reliable control. Live phishing panels, OAuth device code abuse, and real-time OTP interception have rendered password-plus-code authentication useless. Coupled with a surge in physical infiltration tactics by extortion groups, the perimeter is irrelevant. Security teams must immediately pivot to phishing-resistant authentication, continuous threat exposure management, and developer-centric supply chain verification. Reactive patching and static MFA are no longer sufficient to stop coordinated, AI-accelerated campaigns.

threat_stories.headline: AI Compresses the Exploit Lifecycle to Negative Values
threat_stories.narrative:
The era of predictable vulnerability management is over. Anthropic's Project Glasswing, leveraging the Claude Mythos AI model, autonomously identified over 10,000 high- and critical-severity vulnerabilities across 1,000 open-source projects in a single month [17, 64]. This is not a theoretical exercise; the AI demonstrated the ability to discover, analyze, and chain legacy flaws into multi-stage attacks without human direction, compressing exploit development from weeks to hours [116]. The Linux kernel is already feeling the pressure. A cluster of local privilege escalation vulnerabilities abusing the page cache abstraction—Dirty Frag, Copy Fail, and Fragnesia—were disclosed in rapid succession, driven entirely by AI-accelerated code analysis [10, 28]. The mean time to exploit has officially dropped below zero, meaning functional exploits are deployed in the wild before maintainers can publish patches.

This acceleration has triggered a regulatory emergency. India's CERT-In issued a directive mandating the patching or mitigation of critical internet-facing vulnerabilities within 12 hours, explicitly citing AI-assisted automated exploitation as the catalyst [63, 129]. The guidance acknowledges that full patching is often impossible within the window, forcing defenders to rely on temporary network isolation and service disablement. The implication is clear: vulnerability management must shift from periodic scanning to continuous, real-time exposure mapping. Organizations clinging to monthly patch cycles are operating with a guaranteed window of compromise.

The patching bottleneck is now the critical failure point. While AI can generate thousands of vulnerability reports, human capacity for triage, testing, and deployment remains the limiting factor. Only 75 of 530 disclosed bugs from Project Glasswing have been patched, with maintainers reporting severe capacity constraints [64]. Until enterprises implement automated backporting, AI-assisted dependency hardening, and strict runtime isolation, they will remain exposed to a relentless wave of machine-speed exploitation.

timeline.event: Anthropic Project Glasswing and Linux kernel LPE cluster disclosures reveal AI-driven vulnerability discovery at scale.
timeline.significance: Confirms AI has compressed TTE to negative values, making pre-patch exploitation the norm.

timeline.event: CERT-In issues advisory mandating 12-hour patching/mitigation for internet-facing crown jewel systems.
timeline.significance: Regulatory acknowledgment that legacy patch SLAs are obsolete against AI-assisted attacks.

timeline.event: CERT-In formalizes the 12-hour containment window, emphasizing temporary isolation over full patching.
timeline.significance: Forces enterprises to implement compensating controls and continuous exposure management.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: All organizations relying on open-source dependencies, internet-facing infrastructure, or standard 30-day patch cycles face immediate, systemic risk. AI-driven scanners will continuously identify and weaponize unpatched flaws before human defenders can respond.
threat_stories.action_required: Implement Continuous Threat Exposure Management (CTEM) to map attack paths in real-time. Deploy automated dependency scanning with AI-assisted backporting. Enforce strict network segmentation and runtime application self-protection (RASP) for internet-facing assets. Replace legacy patch SLAs with risk-based, exploitation-intelligence-driven prioritization.

threat_stories.headline: The Developer Supply Chain Implosion
threat_stories.narrative:
Threat actors have abandoned perimeter breaches in favor of poisoning the tools developers use to build software. The Megalodon campaign abused compromised Personal Access Tokens to push malicious commits directly into over 5,500 public GitHub repositories, injecting base64-encoded bash payloads into GitHub Actions workflow files [29, 75]. These payloads triggered during CI/CD runs to harvest cloud credentials, SSH keys, and OIDC tokens, exfiltrating them to attacker-controlled infrastructure. Simultaneously, attackers executed a sophisticated Git tag poisoning attack against the Laravel-Lang Composer packages, rewriting over 700 historical tags to point to malicious fork commits [82]. When developers updated dependencies, the autoloader executed a cross-platform PHP information stealer that harvested AWS, Azure, and GCP credentials before exfiltration.

The compromise of developer ecosystems is now industrialized. CISA added multiple supply chain flaws to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, including trojanized DAEMON Tools Lite installers, 42 compromised @tanstack npm packages, and a malicious Nx Console VS Code extension [198]. The GlassWorm botnet, recently disrupted by CrowdStrike and Google, operated on the same principle: distributing trojanized npm and Python packages to harvest credentials and pivot into enterprise environments [153, 212]. These campaigns prove that package registries and CI/CD pipelines are no longer trusted boundaries.

The attack surface has shifted from production servers to developer workstations and build runners. Threat actors exploit weak identity verification, misconfigured pull_request_target triggers, and cache poisoning to achieve persistent access. Organizations that treat supply chain security as a post-build scan are fundamentally misaligned with reality. Verification must occur at commit time, with strict artifact signing, isolated CI runners, and mandatory dependency integrity checks before code reaches production.

timeline.event: Attackers rewrite 700+ Git tags across Laravel-Lang repositories, pointing to malicious forks.
timeline.significance: Demonstrates Git tag poisoning as a reliable supply chain delivery mechanism.

timeline.event: Megalodon campaign compromises 5,500+ GitHub repos via injected CI/CD workflow payloads.
timeline.significance: Confirms CI/CD pipelines are primary targets for credential and secret exfiltration.

timeline.event: CrowdStrike and Google disrupt GlassWorm botnet infrastructure targeting npm/Python ecosystems.
timeline.significance: Validates the scale of open-source package poisoning campaigns.

timeline.event: CISA adds DAEMON Tools, TanStack, and Nx Console flaws to KEV catalog.
timeline.significance: Official recognition of developer tooling as a critical exploitation vector.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Software development teams, CI/CD infrastructure, and any organization consuming third-party packages face severe credential theft and persistent compromise risk. Build environments are now primary attack surfaces.
threat_stories.action_required: Enforce strict branch protection rules requiring PR reviews for all workflow changes. Implement artifact signing and cryptographic hash verification for all dependencies. Isolate CI/CD runners with micro-perimeters and rotate OIDC tokens frequently. Deploy local-first dependency scanning during development, not just in CI pipelines.

threat_stories.headline: MFA is Dead: The Industrialization of Live Phishing
threat_stories.narrative:
Multifactor authentication has been systematically neutralized by live phishing-as-a-service platforms. The FBI issued urgent warnings regarding the Kali365 phishing kit, which bypasses MFA by tricking victims into entering legitimate Microsoft OAuth device codes on spoofed landing pages [43, 60, 101]. This grants attackers valid access and refresh tokens, allowing persistent access to Microsoft 365 services without triggering additional MFA challenges. Attackers leverage these tokens to register trusted devices, create malicious inbox rules to suppress security alerts, and exfiltrate data across Outlook, Teams, and OneDrive.

The Chinese-language PhaaS ecosystem has taken MFA bypass to an operational level. Using AI-powered generators like Puppeteer, operators dynamically clone legitimate login portals to evade signature-based detection [23, 209]. Phishing links are delivered via encrypted channels like RCS and iMessage, bypassing carrier-side filtering. When a victim submits credentials, a live administrative panel relays the data to an attacker who simultaneously triggers an OTP request on their own device. The attacker captures the code seconds before expiration, bypassing MFA entirely and provisioning victim payment cards into attacker-controlled digital wallets for tokenized financial theft.

Password-plus-code authentication is functionally obsolete. The combination of OAuth abuse, real-time OTP interception, and AI-generated phishing infrastructure has created an unstoppable account takeover pipeline. Organizations relying on SMS, authenticator apps, or push notifications are handing attackers direct access. The only viable defense is a complete migration to phishing-resistant authentication standards like FIDO2/WebAuthn, combined with continuous access evaluation and strict OAuth device code flow restrictions.

timeline.event: FBI warns of Kali365 phishing service targeting Microsoft 365 accounts via OAuth device code abuse.
timeline.significance: Confirms MFA bypass at scale through legitimate authentication protocol manipulation.

timeline.event: Security researchers document real-time OTP interception and digital wallet tokenization via PhaaS platforms.
timeline.significance: Demonstrates complete MFA neutralization and financial monetization pipeline.

timeline.event: Malwarebytes analyzes Kali365 kit, confirming widespread MFA bypass and token theft capabilities.
timeline.significance: Validates that traditional MFA controls are actively defeated in production environments.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: All organizations using SMS, TOTP, or push-based MFA face immediate account takeover risk. Financial institutions, healthcare providers, and enterprises relying on Microsoft 365 are experiencing persistent, undetectable compromises.
threat_stories.action_required: Immediately block or restrict Microsoft OAuth device code authentication flows via Conditional Access policies. Migrate critical accounts to FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware security keys. Implement continuous access evaluation and device fingerprinting. Deploy on-device security controls for RCS/iMessage and enforce out-of-band verification for sensitive operations.

threat_stories.headline: Physical Extortion Bypasses the Perimeter Entirely
threat_stories.narrative:
Extortion groups have abandoned complex malware in favor of physical infiltration and social engineering. The FBI's Silent Ransom Group operates by impersonating IT support personnel, contacting employees via phone or phishing emails to address fabricated technical issues [105, 126, 142, 159, 189]. When remote access is denied, operatives physically visit target locations, convincing staff to insert malicious USB storage devices for 'imaging' or 'backup.' Once connected, the devices install remote access software or keyloggers, granting attackers unrestricted network access.

The attack chain deliberately bypasses traditional cybersecurity controls. Actors leverage physical access to escalate privileges, pivot through internal networks, and exfiltrate data using legitimate tools like WinSCP or Rclone to external cloud storage [189]. The stolen data fuels extortion campaigns, with attackers threatening public disclosure or dark web sales. Law firms, financial institutions, and corporate offices are primary targets, with attackers exploiting human trust and inadequate physical security protocols.

This tactic exposes a critical blind spot in modern security architectures. Network segmentation, EDR solutions, and zero-trust frameworks are irrelevant when attackers walk through the front door with a USB drive and a convincing story. Physical security and digital security operate in silos, creating a massive vulnerability. Organizations must treat physical access as a cyber threat vector, enforcing strict visitor verification, disabling unauthorized USB ports, and mandating multi-factor verification for all IT support requests.

timeline.event: FBI issues flash warnings regarding Silent Ransom Group operatives visiting US law firms to steal data via USB.
timeline.significance: Official recognition of physical infiltration as a primary extortion vector.

timeline.event: CSO Online analyzes tech support impersonation campaigns, confirming widespread employee compliance.
timeline.significance: Validates social engineering as the most reliable initial access method.

timeline.event: FBI details USB drop tactics and data exfiltration using legitimate transfer utilities.
timeline.significance: Confirms bypass of network defenses through physical access and authorized tool abuse.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Organizations with open office environments, weak visitor management, or permissive USB policies face direct physical compromise risk. Law firms, financial services, and corporate headquarters are actively targeted.
threat_stories.action_required: Implement strict USB port restrictions and device control policies across all workstations. Enforce verified IT support authentication procedures with out-of-band verification. Deploy EDR solutions to monitor for unauthorized remote access tool installations and anomalous data transfers. Integrate physical security protocols with cyber incident response playbooks.

threat_stories.headline: State Actors Weaponize AI: GreyVibe and Nimbus Manticore
threat_stories.narrative:
Nation-state threat groups are fully integrating AI into their operational workflows, blurring the line between cybercrime and advanced persistent threats. The Russia-linked GreyVIBE group utilizes ChatGPT, Gemini, and Ideogram to develop custom malware, generate phishing lures, and manage attack infrastructure [210, 245, 261, 271]. Their LegionRelay malware and five distinct attack chains target Ukrainian military, government, and civilian sectors. Despite AI assistance, the group exhibits critical OpSec failures, including design flaws in their RAT, test sample uploads to VirusTotal, and informal development artifact naming, allowing researchers to trace command-and-control activity.

Iranian threat actor Nimbus Manticore has similarly expanded its arsenal with AI-assisted malware development. The group deploys MiniFast and MiniJunk V2 via career-themed phishing, trojanized Zoom installers, and SEO poisoning targeting fake Oracle SQL Developer download pages [54, 55, 72, 148]. The malware leverages .NET AppDomain hijacking to silently load rogue DLLs under trusted processes and monitors legitimate Zoom scheduled tasks to establish persistence. The code exhibits AI-generated patterns, featuring verbose error handling, modular structure, and advanced RAT capabilities including privilege escalation and process management.

These campaigns demonstrate that AI is no longer a novelty for state actors; it is a core force multiplier. AI accelerates malware development, automates infrastructure setup, and generates high-fidelity social engineering lures at scale. Defenders must adapt by implementing AI-driven behavioral analytics, strict browser security policies, and advanced email filtering tuned for AI-generated content. The operational tempo of state-sponsored attacks has permanently increased.

timeline.event: Check Point Research documents Nimbus Manticore deploying AI-assisted malware via fake Zoom installers.
timeline.significance: Confirms Iranian APT integration of AI for malware development and delivery.

timeline.event: SecurityWeek and BleepingComputer report GreyVIBE using ChatGPT and Gemini to power cyberattacks.
timeline.significance: Validates Russia-linked group reliance on AI for full campaign lifecycle.

timeline.event: WithSecure and SecurityAffairs publish detailed analysis of GreyVIBE's AI-powered attack chains.
timeline.significance: Exposes AI-driven operational patterns and persistent OpSec vulnerabilities.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Ukrainian infrastructure, US financial firms, and aviation/software companies face targeted, AI-accelerated espionage and sabotage campaigns. Traditional signature-based detection is ineffective against dynamically generated AI malware.
threat_stories.action_required: Deploy advanced email security gateways with AI-phishing detection. Implement strict web filtering to block malicious domains and fake CAPTCHA redirects. Enforce EDR solutions tuned for custom RATs, PowerShell abuse, and AppDomain hijacking. Conduct regular security awareness training focusing on AI-generated lures and fake software downloads.

actor_profiles.name: GREYVIBE
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Deployed AI-generated phishing lures, custom LegionRelay RAT, and five attack chains (PhantomMail, PhantomClick, PrincessClub, DroneLink, Nebo) targeting Ukrainian military and government sectors. Utilized ChatGPT, Gemini, and Ideogram for malware development and infrastructure setup.

actor_profiles.name: Nimbus Manticore
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Deployed MiniFast and MiniJunk V2 malware via trojanized Zoom installers, SEO poisoning, and career-themed phishing. Leveraged .NET AppDomain hijacking and scheduled task manipulation for persistence. AI-assisted malware development observed.

actor_profiles.name: ShinyHunters
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Executed social engineering campaigns to compromise employee credentials at Carnival Corporation and Charter Communications. Exfiltrated personal data for 6 million and 4.9 million individuals respectively, leveraging pure extortion tactics without ransomware encryption.

actor_profiles.name: Kali365 Operators
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Operated live phishing-as-a-service platform bypassing MFA via OAuth device code abuse and real-time OTP interception. Delivered phishing links via RCS/iMessage, utilized AI-generated pages, and provisioned victim payment cards into attacker digital wallets.

actor_profiles.name: Silent Ransom Group
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Conducted physical infiltration campaigns targeting US law firms and corporate offices. Impersonated IT support to gain physical access, deployed malicious USB devices for remote access installation, and exfiltrated data via WinSCP/Rclone for extortion.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: SQL injection flaw in Drupal's database query sanitization API allows unauthenticated attackers to inject arbitrary SQL commands on PostgreSQL-backed sites. Successful exploitation leads to information disclosure, privilege escalation, and remote code execution.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply the critical security patch released by Drupal on May 20, 2026. Federal agencies must remediate by May 27, 2026, per CISA BOD 22-01.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Incorrect privilege assignment in the Redis enable/disable functions allows any authenticated cPanel user to execute arbitrary scripts with root-level permissions. The flaw enables full server compromise without complex conditions.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade the LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin to version 2.4.7 or later immediately. Review cPanel logs for suspicious Redis-related API calls.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Hardcoded, identical ASP.NET machineKey values across deployments enable ViewState deserialization attacks. Attackers craft malicious __VIEWSTATE payloads to trigger unauthenticated remote code execution and deploy Godzilla web shells.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Generate and deploy a unique, cryptographically strong machine key for each instance. Apply vendor patch and rotate all credentials.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: SQL injection vulnerability in the Content API allows unauthenticated attackers to read arbitrary database contents, steal Admin API keys, and inject malicious JavaScript payloads for ClickFix ad-fraud campaigns.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Immediately update Ghost CMS to the latest patched version. Restrict Content API access via firewall or WAF rules if patching is delayed.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Improper access control allows unauthenticated attackers to bypass authentication via API endpoints. Specially crafted HTTP requests are processed as administrative actions, enabling RCE and deployment of the EKZ Infostealer.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Install hotfixes for FortiClient EMS 7.4.5/7.4.6 immediately, or upgrade to version 7.4.7. Restrict console access to trusted IPs.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Authentication bypass vulnerability caused by improper Host header validation. Malformed Host headers shift path parsing boundaries, tricking access control middleware into granting access to protected routes.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade to Starlette version 1.0.1 or later. Deploy a compliant reverse proxy to filter malformed Host headers before they reach the backend.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Legacy remote code execution flaw autonomously discovered and exploited by AI. The vulnerability remains unpatched for 17 years and enables network-based RCE without authentication.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply vendor patches immediately. Restrict NFS access to trusted networks only and implement zero-trust network architecture.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Cluster of six unpatched Windows zero-day vulnerabilities publicly disclosed without vendor coordination. Working POC code published; three flaws (BlueHammer, RedSun, UnDefend) already weaponized in the wild.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply Microsoft security updates immediately upon release. Implement strict network segmentation and application whitelisting to limit propagation.

hunting_leads.title: GitHub Actions Workflow Injection (Megalodon)
hunting_leads.context: Attackers abuse compromised PATs to push base64-encoded bash payloads into CI/CD workflow files, triggering secret exfiltration during builds.
hunting_leads.query: | where RepositoryId in (affected_repos) | where CommitMessage contains "SysDiag" or CommitMessage contains "Optimize-Build" | where WorkflowFile contains "base64" or WorkflowFile contains "curl" | summarize count() by CommitSha, CommitMessage, WorkflowFile

hunting_leads.title: Laravel-Lang Git Tag Poisoning
hunting_leads.context: Over 700 historical Git tags rewritten to point to malicious forks. Composer autoloader executes PHP dropper harvesting cloud credentials.
hunting_leads.query: | where PackageName in ("laravel-lang/lang", "laravel-lang/http-statuses", "laravel-lang/attributes") | where CommitDate > "2026-05-20" | where TagRewritten == true | summarize count() by PackageName, CommitSha, ForkUrl

hunting_leads.title: OAuth Device Code Flow Abuse (Kali365)
hunting_leads.context: Victims enter legitimate Microsoft OAuth device codes on spoofed pages, granting attackers valid refresh tokens and bypassing MFA entirely.
hunting_leads.query: | where AuthMethod == "OAuthDeviceCode" | where IPAddress not in (trusted_networks) | where SessionDuration > 3600 | where DeviceRegistration == true | summarize count() by UserId, IPAddress, DeviceId

hunting_leads.title: FortiClient EMS EKZ Infostealer Deployment
hunting_leads.context: Attackers exploit CVE-2026-35616 to push malicious PowerShell commands disguised as Fortinet updates, deploying EKZ credential stealer.
hunting_leads.query: | where SourceSystem == "FortiClientEMS" | where ActionType == "PolicyPush" or ActionType == "EndpointUpdate" | where Payload contains "PowerShell" or Payload contains "EKZ" | where TargetEndpoint not in (expected_deployment_groups) | summarize count() by EMSInstanceId, TargetEndpoint, PayloadHash

hunting_leads.title: ChatGPhish Markdown Prompt Injection
hunting_leads.context: ChatGPT web interface blindly renders attacker-controlled Markdown from external pages during summarization, enabling phishing link injection.
hunting_leads.query: | where Application == "ChatGPT" | where Action == "PageSummary" | where OutputContainsMarkdown == true | where ExternalLinkDetected == true | summarize count() by UserId, SourceUrl, RenderedLink
indicators: Spoofed security alerts in chat output

top_vulnerabilities: {'summary': 'Unpatched vulnerability in Chromium-based browsers allowing persistent JavaScript execution via Service Workers and Background Fetch API, enabling browser hijacking for DDoS, crypto mining, and tracking.', 'severity': 'high', 'affected_products': ['Chromium', 'Google Chrome', 'Microsoft Edge', 'Opera'], 'complexity': 'low', 'cvss_score': 0, 'workarounds': ['Disable Service Workers and Background Fetch API via browser flags or settings', 'Use browser extensions to block background scripts and trackers', 'Monitor and restrict browser background activity', 'Avoid visiting untrusted or suspicious websites'], 'references': ['https://www.csoonline.com/article/google-leaks-details-for-chromium-bug-that-can-turn-browsers-into-bots', "Lyra Rebane's public bug report and Mastodon disclosure"], 'integrity_impact': 'high', 'description': 'The vulnerability abuses the Service Worker feature and Background Fetch API in Chromium-based browsers. By creating and aborting background fetches every 20 seconds, an attacker can bypass the limited lifespan of a Service Worker, keeping it active indefinitely even after browser restarts. This allows persistent execution of JavaScript code, which can be leveraged for user tracking (accessing timestamps, IP, User-Agent), remote payload execution, side-channel attacks, WebAssembly crypto miners, and DDoS amplification. UI visibility of the downloads varies across browser versions and may be completely invisible in recent builds.', 'attack_vector': 'network', 'privileges_required': 'none', 'exploit_maturity': 'proof-of-concept', 'availability_impact': 'high', 'remediation': 'Apply official patches from the Chromium project and browser vendors once released. A fundamental fix requires updating the Background Fetch API specification to enforce hard time limits for Service Worker termination.', 'exploit_available': 'yes', 'cve_id': '', 'cvss_vector': '', 'user_interaction': 'required', 'scope': 'changed', 'confidentiality_impact': 'low', 'published_date': '2026-05-23', 'discovery_date': ''}

{'summary': 'Phishing campaign leveraging a trusted Ukrainian learning platform as bait to deliver malicious JavaScript payloads via PDF attachments, exploiting default Windows script execution capabilities and user interaction.', 'severity': 'high', 'affected_products': ['Microsoft Windows', 'PDF Reader Applications', 'Web Browsers'], 'complexity': 'low', 'cvss_score': '', 'workarounds': ['Disable Windows Script Host (wscript.exe) for non-administrative users', 'Configure email gateways to strip or sandbox PDF attachments', 'Block outbound HTTP POST requests to unknown .icu domains', 'Monitor and alert on eval() usage in downloaded JavaScript files', 'Restrict ZIP archive auto-extraction in browsers and email clients'], 'references': ['CERT-UA Advisory on Ghostwriter Campaign', 'SecurityAffairs: Ghostwriter Is Back, Using a Ukrainian Learning Platform as Bait', 'FireEye Report on GhostWriter Disinformation Campaign (2020)', 'SentinelLABS Report on Ghostwriter PicassoLoader Campaign (2024)'], 'integrity_impact': 'high', 'description': 'The Ghostwriter APT group (UAC-0057/UNC1151) executes a multi-stage delivery chain beginning with phishing emails sent from compromised accounts. The emails contain PDF attachments with embedded links that, when clicked, download a ZIP archive containing a JavaScript file named OYSTERFRESH. This script simultaneously displays a decoy document to avoid suspicion and silently drops an obfuscated payload (OYSTERBLUES) into the Windows Registry. It also downloads a decoder component (OYSTERSHUCK) that uses string reversal, ROT13, and URL decoding to launch OYSTERBLUES. Once active, OYSTERBLUES profiles the system (hostname, username, OS version, boot time, running processes) and exfiltrates data via HTTP POST to a C2 server. It awaits further instructions executed dynamically via the eval() function. The chain culminates in the deployment of Cobalt Strike for persistent post-exploitation access. The attack heavily relies on default Windows configurations that allow standard users to execute wscript.exe without restriction.', 'attack_vector': 'network', 'privileges_required': 'low', 'exploit_maturity': 'high', 'availability_impact': 'low', 'remediation': 'Restrict execution of wscript.exe for standard user accounts via Group Policy, AppLocker, or WDAC. Implement strict email filtering, attachment sandboxing, and PDF security controls. Block network traffic to suspicious TLDs (.icu) and monitor Cloudflare-hosted domains associated with threat infrastructure. Deploy EDR/XDR solutions to detect registry persistence, dynamic JavaScript execution, and Cobalt Strike artifacts.', 'exploit_available': 'yes', 'cve_id': '', 'cvss_vector': '', 'user_interaction': 'required', 'scope': 'changed', 'confidentiality_impact': 'high', 'published_date': '2026-05-23', 'discovery_date': ''}
statistics.key_changes: AI-driven vulnerability discovery has compressed TTE to negative values, forcing regulatory mandates like CERT-In's 12-hour patching window. Supply chain attacks have shifted from end-user apps to developer tooling and CI/CD pipelines. MFA bypass via live phishing panels and OAuth abuse has rendered traditional 2FA obsolete.
emerging_trends: AI-assisted malware development and lure generation by state actors
Git tag poisoning and CI/CD workflow injection as primary supply chain vectors
Physical infiltration and social engineering replacing complex malware for extortion
Continuous Threat Exposure Management replacing legacy patch SLAs
declining_threats: Traditional ransomware encryption campaigns in favor of pure data extortion
Signature-based phishing detection due to AI-generated dynamic pages
Static vulnerability scanning due to AI-driven exploit acceleration
